text: Buying is more cost-effective than building, and it has the highest return.
The case is strong. Now the board asks: can you say it in two minutes?
Recommendation
Rationale
Concise
Compelling

Endorse
Upshot

What the board needs is one number, the payback.
It is the return that pays for the upfront cost.
What matters here is the rationale, not the slides.
The reason we should buy is the quicker payback.
My recommendation is to buy. The rationale is concise: the highest return, the quickest payback.
That is fast. But I have ten slides in front of me. What do you actually want from this board?
What the board needs is one number: the payback. The slides are detail; the payback is the case.
The upfront cost is high. Convince me before I raise it with the others.
It is the return that pays for that cost. I advocate for buying, and I reinforce it: the case is compelling.
Good. Give me the upshot in one line, and the board will endorse it.
The upshot is simple: buying is the smarter spend, so we approve the budget and we move.
My recommendation is to buy from the vendor.
I advocate for buying, and the case is compelling.

Carlos, you have the business case. Now you have to present it. The board does not want the ten-slide version; they want the recommendation in two minutes. Let me give you the structure of a strong pitch. Open with the recommendation, not the background. What you say first is what they remember, so the first sentence is: my recommendation is to buy. Then give the rationale, the reasons, but keep it concise. Two or three reasons, no more. The reason it is more cost-effective is the quicker payback. The reason we should buy is the higher return. Use emphasis to point at the one number that matters. What the board needs is a single number, the payback, so say it like that: what matters here is the payback. Then anticipate the objection and reinforce your point before they raise it. They will say the upfront cost is high, so you say: yes, it is the upfront cost that worries the board, but it is the return that pays for it. Close with the upshot, the one-line conclusion. In short, buying is the smarter spend. The upshot is simple: we approve the budget and we move. A compelling pitch is not louder, it is clearer. You advocate for one option, you give the rationale, and you end with the upshot. If you can say it in two minutes, the board will endorse it. If it takes ten, they will ask for another meeting.

Hi Carlos, this is Diana, calling after your pitch to the directors. I want to give you honest feedback, because it was good and it can be better. First, the opening. You led with the recommendation, which is exactly right. The reason that worked is that the board knew, in one sentence, what you were asking for. Keep doing that. Second, the rationale. You gave three reasons, and that was one too many. The most compelling pitch is the most concise one, so next time give two reasons, the two that matter most, and stop. Third, the emphasis. When you said what the board needs is a quicker payback, every director looked up. That is the moment that landed. Do more of that. Use a cleft sentence to point at the one thing: what matters is the payback, it is the return that pays for the cost. Fourth, the close. Your upshot was a little soft. Do not end on the downside; end on the recommendation. The upshot should be one clear line: buying is the smarter spend, so we approve it. One more thing. When the lead director pushed back on the price, you reinforced your point well, but you apologized first. Do not apologize for the upfront cost. Advocate for the return. The directors are ready to endorse the recommendation, but they want the two-minute version, not the ten-minute one. Tighten it, lead with the recommendation, and close on the upshot. Send me the final version and we present to the full board on Thursday. Strong work, Carlos. Talk soon.

First, open with the recommendation. Then give a concise rationale, two reasons. Next, use an emphasis frame on the one number that matters. After that, reinforce your point before the objection. Finally, close with the upshot in one line.
What the board needs is one number: the payback.
It is the return that pays for the upfront cost.
I advocate for buying, and the case is compelling.
